localBiteReport: The most recent available captain reports from Choctawhatchee Bay (dated roughly 6–14 days ago via captainexperiences.com) show speckled trout as the standout inshore bite right now, with a Santa Rosa Beach guide reporting a strong trout bite and trout scattered but feeding actively across the bay. Bull redfish are also showing well — one angler out of Santa Rosa Beach recently fought a 40-inch, 26-lb bull red. Windy conditions over the past couple of weeks have pushed anglers to be selective about where they set up, but guides note that as winds settle more areas should open up. Water temp at this location is reading 85°F today. Note: the most recent inshore-focused reports found are approximately 6–14 days old — no reports dated within the past 3–4 days were publicly available at time of search.

aiSummary: Speckled trout are the standout inshore bite across Choctawhatchee Bay right now, with guides reporting fish scattered but feeding actively bay-wide, and bull redfish also showing well with a recent 40-inch, 26-lb fish reported out of Santa Rosa Beach. Get on the water early — a 68% storm chance kicks in after 11 AM, making a pre-noon exit plan essential on a day with a high near 90°F and heat index up to 100°F. ENE winds at 10–15 mph will favor protected bayous over open-water spots, so choose your location accordingly.

bridge.aiNote: The 331 Bridge spans the narrow neck of Choctawhatchee Bay, creating a current funnel that pushes bait through on both tides. Pilings hold speckled trout, redfish, sheepshead, and black drum year-round, with the lee-side pilings serving as the prime ambush zone on incoming tides. Being open water, this is typically the first spot to become unfishable when wind and chop build.
bridge.todaysCall: With ENE winds running 10–15 mph, expect chop on the open water around the bridge — fish the lee-side pilings to get out of the wind and put yourself where trout and reds will be ambushing bait pushed by the current. Low tide hits around 10:11 AM, so the early morning window before that offers the best tidal movement to work the pilings effectively. Given the 68% storm chance after 11 AM, plan to be off this exposed stretch well before midday — there is no shelter here when weather builds. Slightly stained water means fish are orienting tightly to structure, so keep baits close to the pilings rather than fishing open water.

alaqua.aiNote: Alaqua Bayou offers protected water with a strong year-round redfish population, plus speckled trout and flounder near the mouth. The oyster bars at the creek mouth are the prime target, and tree cover keeps water temperatures a few degrees cooler than the open bay while providing shelter from wind and weather. The upper creek transitions to brackish water and holds bass alongside smaller reds.
alaqua.todaysCall: Alaqua is a smart call today — tree cover and protected water make it one of the more comfortable options with ENE winds at 10–15 mph and storms likely after 11 AM. Hit the oyster bars at the creek mouth during the falling tide toward the 10:11 AM low; bait will be funneling out and reds and trout should both be feeding. The slightly stained water favors noisy or darker soft plastics and rattling corks to help fish locate your presentation. With the storm window opening late morning, you can fish comfortably here and duck out early with minimal run time to the ramp.

basin.aiNote: Basin Bayou sees lighter fishing pressure than Alaqua and LaGrange, which means less-educated fish and a more consistent sight-fishing opportunity. The interior grass flats are classic shallow-water territory for redfish in under two feet of water, and pompano occasionally show at the mouth on moving tides in late spring and early summer. It offers less weather protection than LaGrange when conditions deteriorate.
basin.todaysCall: Basin Bayou's lighter pressure makes it worth the trip today if you want a less-crowded shot at reds on the interior grass flats — the falling tide toward the 10:11 AM low will concentrate fish on flat edges and along drop-offs. Start at the mouth early when tidal movement is still active, then push inside to sight-fish shallow reds as the tide bottoms out. The slightly stained water will limit true sight-fishing clarity, so rely on reading wakes and nervous water rather than spotting fish visually. Keep a close eye on the sky after 11 AM — Basin offers less protection than LaGrange, so have a clear exit plan before storms develop.

lagrange.aiNote: LaGrange Bayou is the top trout producer in this area of the bay, with legendary oyster bars at the mouth and interior grass flats that hold redfish year-round. Deep holes inside hold black drum and sheepshead, making it one of the most species-diverse spots on this dashboard. It is also the most protected water on this side of the bay, making it the default choice when weather builds.
lagrange.todaysCall: LaGrange is the best overall option today — it aligns perfectly with the active trout bite reported bay-wide and provides the most protection from ENE winds and incoming afternoon storms. Work the oyster bars at the mouth hard during the falling tide to the 10:11 AM low; this is where trout and reds should be stacking up as bait gets pushed off the flats. Slightly stained water actually works in your favor on the oyster bars — trout will be tight to structure and a gold spoon, paddle tail, or popping cork rig near the bars should draw strikes. If storms push in earlier than expected, move inside to the deeper holes for black drum and sheepshead and wait it out — this is the safest spot on the bay to do exactly that.

fourmile.aiNote: Four Mile Creek is a tidal creek running between Shipyard Marina and the bay, with tidal flow pushing baitfish up the creek on incoming tides and concentrating redfish and trout in the bends and along grass edges. The creek mouth is a prime flounder zone, and the upper section transitions to brackish water where bass join smaller reds. Short and well-protected, it is the safest fishery on the dashboard when weather threatens.
fourmile.todaysCall: Four Mile Creek is an excellent choice today for anglers who want to stay close to the marina and keep weather risk low — it is the most protected and quickest-exit spot on the dashboard with storms likely after 11 AM. The falling tide toward the 10:11 AM low will be the key window; position in the bends and along grass edges as bait moves out and reds and trout set up to feed. The creek mouth is worth a few casts for flounder on the way in or out — a slow-dragged paddle tail on the bottom near the mouth is the right call. With the ENE wind, the creek orientation should provide a comfortable buffer, letting you stay focused on fishing rather than fighting chop.

hogtown.aiNote: Hogtown Bayou is a signature Santa Rosa Beach guide spot and one of the most consistently productive trout flats on the bay. Summer mornings are the peak window, with trout stacking on grass in two to four feet of water, and Spanish mackerel show at the mouth on moving tides. It sits further from the 331 Bridge than other bayous, so factor in run time when planning your day.
hogtown.todaysCall: Hogtown is one of the most directly relevant spots given the active trout bite that Santa Rosa Beach guides have been reporting across the bay — morning low-light hours on the grass flats in two to four feet is exactly the right setup today. The falling tide toward the 10:11 AM low gives you a strong early window; get on the flats well before sunrise at 5:54 AM and work topwater or soft plastics through the grass. The slightly stained water means fish are feeding by feel and vibration as much as sight, so a popping cork or a twitchbait with some noise will help. Factor in the run time from Shipyard Marina, and commit to being off the water or tucked into the bayou before the 11 AM storm window opens — open flats here leave little shelter if weather moves fast.

rocky.aiNote: Rocky Bayou sits inside Fred Gannon Rocky Bayou State Park near Niceville and is significantly less pressured than the bayous closer to the 331 Bridge. Freshwater influence from Rocky Creek makes it an excellent flounder fishery, and the glass-calm state park coves are ideal for sight-fishing redfish in shallow water. Mangrove snapper hold near dock pilings, and this is the furthest location from Shipyard Marina — run time is a real factor.
rocky.todaysCall: Rocky Bayou's protected coves and low pressure make it an appealing escape on a stormy day, but factor in the run time carefully — being the furthest location from Shipyard Marina, you need to build in extra buffer before the post-11 AM storm window. If you commit to going, fish the shallow coves early for reds during the falling tide toward the 10:11 AM low; the state park setting should offer glass-calm water regardless of the ENE wind. Slightly stained water reduces sight-fishing effectiveness, so focus on reading movement and structure edges rather than spotting tailing fish. The dock pilings are worth a few casts for mangrove snapper, and a slow-dragged bait near structure will also put you in front of flounder throughout the tidal drop.
